We know that integrated systems with interdependent parts come from planners who envisioned the whole before assembling the parts. The inference to design is not a retreat from knowledge. It is a conclusion drawn from the most universal pattern in our experience.

The fossil record, honestly read, tells the same story. The Cambrian explosion presents us with virtually every major animal body plan appearing suddenly, fully formed, without the long ancestral fuse Darwin himself admitted his theory required. Where are the innumerable transitional forms his theory predicted? The strata are silent. What we find instead is stasis and sudden appearance—precisely what we would expect if organisms were created as integrated kinds rather than gradually morphing from common ancestors.

Nor does the genetic evidence rescue the standard account. Shared similarities between species are routinely offered as proof of common descent, but shared similarities are equally the signature of common design. Engineers reuse successful components across different products. The presence of a wheel on a car and a wheel on a cart is not evidence that one descended from the other; it is evidence that wheels work. Homology demonstrates design economy, not ancestry.

I do not write this to wound my colleagues, many of whom I respect. I write it because I have looked at the cell, and I have seen what is there. The machinery is too exquisite. The information is too specified. The integration is too precise. One may call the Designer by whatever name one prefers, or decline to name Him at all. But to stand before the architecture of the living cell and insist that no architect was involved requires a commitment to philosophical materialism that no amount of evidence could ever disturb. # In the Beginning: A Manifesto on Creation

*An essay by Pastor John Whitfield*

For forty years I have stood in the pulpit of Grace Covenant Church, and for forty years I have watched a generation grow up being told that they are accidents. Cosmic accidents. Biological accidents. The unintended byproducts of mindless chemistry churning for billions of years in some forgotten pond. I have buried the despair this doctrine produces. I have counseled the young people it has hollowed out. And I have come to believe, with every fiber of my being, that the theory of evolution by natural selection is not only wrong — it is one of the greatest intellectual errors of the modern age. Life was created, deliberately and completely, by the hand of a personal God, and the evidence of that creation is written into every living thing.

Consider first the question that every honest thinker must ask: where does order come from? In every corner of human experience, order requires a mind. A watch implies a watchmaker. A cathedral implies an architect. A sonnet implies a poet. No one, walking through the halls of the Louvre, imagines that the paintings assembled themselves through a long series of accidental paint splatters, each one slightly better than the last, preserved by some unseen curator. We recognize design because design bears the unmistakable signature of intention. And yet we are asked to believe that the human body — a structure more intricate than any cathedral, more elegant than any sonnet, more precise than any watch — assembled itself through a blind, purposeless process. I cannot accept this. I will not.

Nowhere is the absurdity of this claim more evident than in the human eye. The eye is not a simple organ. It is a marvel of coordinated engineering. It requires a transparent cornea, positioned precisely in front of an adjustable iris, which controls the amount of light passing through a lens of exactly the right curvature, which focuses that light onto a retina lined with over a hundred million photoreceptor cells, which convert photons into electrochemical signals, which travel along the optic nerve to a region of the brain capable of interpreting those signals as vision. Remove any one of these components and the entire system fails. A cornea without a retina is useless. A retina without a lens is useless. A lens without a pupil is useless. An optic nerve without a brain to interpret its signals is useless.

This is what scientists have called *irreducible complexity*, and it is fatal to the theory of natural selection. Darwin's mechanism depends on the gradual accumulation of small, advantageous changes, each preserved because it confers some survival benefit. But a half-formed eye confers no benefit. A light-sensitive patch is not a stepping stone to vision any more than a puddle of ink is a stepping stone to a novel. Each of the eye's components is meaningless without the others. They must exist together, functioning together, from the beginning — or they do not function at all. Darwin himself confessed, in *On the Origin of Species*, that the thought of the eye gave him a cold shudder. He was right to shudder. He had glimpsed the hand of the Creator and recoiled from it.

And the eye is only one example. The human body is layered with such systems. The blood-clotting cascade, a precise sequence of chemical reactions that must occur in the correct order or we bleed to death. The bacterial flagellum, a rotary motor assembled from dozens of proteins that does nothing at all unless all of them are present and arranged correctly. The genetic code itself, a language of astonishing sophistication, complete with error-correction mechanisms that suggest not accident but authorship. Every honest investigation into the machinery of life uncovers not the crude tinkering of chance but the fingerprints of a mind.

The fossil record, too, tells a story quite different from the one we are taught in schools. We are promised transitional forms — the slow, gradual gradient of species melting into one another — and yet the rocks stubbornly refuse to cooperate. The Cambrian explosion shows us not gradual emergence but the sudden appearance of nearly every major body plan, fully formed, without ancestors. The gaps in the fossil record are not gaps. They are the natural shape of a creation that came into being as it is, by divine decree, not through ages of blood-soaked trial and error.

Some will say that faith has no place in a discussion of science. But I would answer that everyone brings faith to this question. The materialist has faith that matter is all there is. The evolutionist has faith that blind processes can produce what has never been observed to arise from blind processes — consciousness, language, moral law, beauty. I simply place my faith where it belongs: in the testimony of Scripture and the evidence of creation itself, which speak with one voice. "The heavens declare the glory of God," the psalmist writes, "and the firmament showeth His handiwork." He wrote those words three thousand years ago, and they remain truer today than any theory ever formulated in any university.

We were not made by accident. We were made on purpose, for a purpose, by One who knew us before we were formed. This is the good news I have preached for forty years, and it is the good news I will preach until the Lord calls me home. The world may call it foolishness. I call it the beginning of wisdom.

*An essay by Pastor John Whitfield*

I have counseled the young people it has hollowed out. Life was created, deliberately and completely, by the hand of a personal God, and the evidence of that creation is written into every living thing.

Consider first the question that every honest thinker must ask: where does order come from? In every corner of human experience, order requires a mind. A watch implies a watchmaker. A cathedral implies an architect. A sonnet implies a poet. No one, walking through the halls of the Louvre, imagines that the paintings assembled themselves through a long series of accidental paint splatters, each one slightly better than the last, preserved by some unseen curator. We recognize design because design bears the unmistakable signature of intention. And yet we are asked to believe that the human body — a structure more intricate than any cathedral, more elegant than any sonnet, more precise than any watch — assembled itself through a blind, purposeless process. I cannot accept this. I will not.

Nowhere is the absurdity of this claim more evident than in the human eye. The eye is not a simple organ. It is a marvel of coordinated engineering. It requires a transparent cornea, positioned precisely in front of an adjustable iris, which controls the amount of light passing through a lens of exactly the right curvature, which focuses that light onto a retina lined with over a hundred million photoreceptor cells, which convert photons into electrochemical signals, which travel along the optic nerve to a region of the brain capable of interpreting those signals as vision. Remove any one of these components and the entire system fails.

This is what scientists have called *irreducible complexity*, and it is fatal to the theory of natural selection. Darwin's mechanism depends on the gradual accumulation of small, advantageous changes, each preserved because it confers some survival benefit. But a half-formed eye confers no benefit. A light-sensitive patch is not a stepping stone to vision any more than a puddle of ink is a stepping stone to a novel. Each of the eye's components is meaningless without the others. Darwin himself confessed, in *On the Origin of Species*, that the thought of the eye gave him a cold shudder. He was right to shudder. He had glimpsed the hand of the Creator and recoiled from it.

And the eye is only one example. The blood-clotting cascade. The bacterial flagellum. The genetic code itself, a language of astonishing sophistication, complete with error-correction mechanisms that suggest not accident but authorship. Every honest investigation into the machinery of life uncovers not the crude tinkering of chance but the fingerprints of a mind.

Now, I know what the professors say next. They say: "Ah, but the earth is billions of years old. Given enough time, anything is possible." Time, they tell us, is the magician that transforms mud into man. But I would ask you, brothers and sisters, to look carefully at how that "time" is measured — because the deeper you dig, the more the whole edifice begins to tremble.

The entire evolutionary timeline rests on radiometric dating, and radiometric dating rests on assumptions — assumptions that the rate of radioactive decay has never changed, that the initial amount of parent and daughter isotopes is known, that no contamination has occurred across millions of years. Every one of those assumptions is an act of faith. And when we test the methods against things we actually know the age of, the results are damning.

Living mollusks pulled from Hawaiian waters have been carbon-dated at over a thousand years old. Seal pups freshly killed in Antarctica have returned ages of thirteen hundred years. In 1949, fresh wood from a living tree was submitted and dated at thousands of years. Lava flows from Mount Ngauruhoe in New Zealand, known to have erupted in the 1950s, were dated by the potassium-argon method at up to 3.5 million years old. Basalt from Mount St. Helens — erupted in our own lifetimes, in 1986 — was dated at 2.8 million years. The creationist geologist Dr. Andrew Snelling has documented case after case of these anomalies, and the secular journals quietly explain them away with talk of "excess argon" or "reservoir effects" — meaning, in plain English, that when the method gives an answer they like, it is reliable; when it gives an answer they don't like, it is contaminated.

I ask you: what kind of science is that? If a ruler measured a known twelve-inch board as six feet long, would we trust that same ruler to measure the distance to the moon? And yet we are expected to stake the entire story of human origins on precisely such a ruler.

The truth, I believe, is that the earth is young — thousands of years, not billions — just as the genealogies of Scripture have always told us. The fossil record confirms it. The Cambrian explosion shows us not gradual emergence but the sudden appearance of nearly every major body plan, fully formed, without ancestors. Soft tissue and red blood cells have been discovered in dinosaur bones supposedly sixty-five million years old — a finding that should have revolutionized paleontology but was instead quietly tucked away, because it cannot be reconciled with the official timeline. Flesh does not last sixty-five million years. It does not last one million. The bones, in their silent way, are preaching the same sermon I am preaching now.

Some will say that faith has no place in a discussion of science. But I would answer that everyone brings faith to this question. The materialist has faith that matter is all there is. The evolutionist has faith that blind processes can produce what has never been observed to arise from blind processes — consciousness, language, moral law, beauty. He has faith that his dating methods are reliable even when they return impossible answers. I simply place my faith where it belongs: in the testimony of Scripture and the evidence of creation itself, which speak with one voice. "The heavens declare the glory of God," the psalmist writes, "and the firmament showeth His handiwork." He wrote those words three thousand years ago, and they remain truer today than any theory ever formulated in any university.

We were not made by accident. We were made on purpose, for a purpose, by One who knew us before we were formed. This is the good news I have preached for forty years, and it is the good news I will preach until the Lord calls me home. The world may call it foolishness. I call it the beginning of wisdom.

---

*— Pastor John Whitfield*

---

The question of how life came to exist on Earth is among the most profound any person can ask. The most immediate evidence for creation is the staggering complexity and apparent purpose found throughout the natural world. The human eye, the bacterial flagellum, the information-rich code of DNA—each of these systems displays the hallmarks of engineering. When we encounter a watch on a beach, as William Paley famously argued, we do not conclude that wind and waves assembled it; we infer a watchmaker. Living systems are incomparably more complex than any watch, and the inference to a Designer is correspondingly stronger.

To follow the evidence wherever it leads is to recognize the fingerprints of a Designer on every living thing. Creation is not merely a religious alternative to evolution—it is the better explanation for the world we actually inhabit.

*A Keynote Address by Dr. Marcus Thorne*

Ladies and gentlemen, colleagues, friends—thank you.

I stand before you to declare a truth that the evidence of every discipline confirms and that the courage of honest inquiry demands we acknowledge: life was created. Deliberately. Purposefully. **Evolution as Naturalistic Religion**

Consider what evolution asks you to believe. It asks you to believe that nothing produced something. That non-life produced life. That chaos produced order. That mindlessness produced mind. That amorality produced morality. Each of these transitions is unobserved, unrepeated, and unexplained—yet we are told that to doubt them is to reject science itself.

My friends, this is not science. Science is the study of what can be observed, tested, and repeated. What cannot be observed, tested, or repeated—what must instead be accepted on the authority of credentialed teachers who assure us it happened long ago where no one can check—this has a different name. Evolution is the creation myth of philosophical naturalism. It begins with an article of faith: that nothing exists beyond matter and energy. It proceeds to a cosmogony: the spontaneous self-organization of the universe. It offers a genesis: life arising unbidden from a warm chemical pond. It preaches a gospel: that humanity is the product of blind forces, accountable to nothing, designed for nothing, destined for nothing. And it anathematizes its dissenters with a zeal that any inquisitor would recognize.

**The Collapse of the Evidence**

Strip away the rhetoric and examine the evidence. The fossil record, which Darwin promised would vindicate his theory, has instead humiliated it. The Cambrian explosion shows the major body plans appearing suddenly, fully formed, without ancestors. Transitional forms—the creatures that should populate the record by the billions—are so rare that Stephen Jay Gould called their absence "the trade secret of paleontology."

Look to the laboratory. Richard Lenski has cultivated *E. coli* for over seventy-five thousand generations—the evolutionary equivalent of more than a million years of human history. The result? *E. coli*. Still *E. coli*. Always *E. coli*. Where are the new body plans? Where are the new organs? Where is the documented emergence of the novel genetic information that the evolutionary story requires at every turn?

It does not exist. No laboratory has ever observed natural processes producing the kind of functional, specified information required to build a new creature. Mutation reshuffles; selection filters. Neither creates. The coded language of DNA—a language more sophisticated than any human tongue—is treated as if it wrote itself. But we know, from every other domain of human experience, that language comes from minds. Information comes from intelligence. Code comes from a coder.

And then there is the witness of physics itself. The Second Law of Thermodynamics tells us what every engineer, every chemist, every honest observer of the natural world already knows: left to themselves, systems move toward disorder. Buildings crumble. Iron rusts. Order decays. The universe runs down.

Yet evolution asks us to believe the opposite story—that matter, left to itself over billions of years, organized itself from hydrogen gas into human brains. That disorder spontaneously became symphonies. That chaos wrote the genetic code. This is not merely improbable. It is a reversal of the most fundamental tendency in nature. Complexity does not build itself. Information does not write itself. Order does not emerge from chaos without an ordering hand. The laws of physics themselves testify against the evolutionary narrative and for the necessity of a Designer.

But the evolutionary religion is not merely false. It is corrosive. What it costs us is not only scientific accuracy but the very foundation of human worth.

If man is merely a rearranged ape, an accident of chemistry, a temporary pattern in a cold universe—then what is human dignity? A pleasant fiction. What are human rights? Arbitrary conventions. What is conscience? A neurological misfire. What is love? A reproductive strategy. What is justice? A tribal preference dressed in robes.

The twentieth century showed us what societies do when they fully believe that man is only an animal. The eugenics movements, the racial hierarchies, the regimes that treated human beings as breeding stock or vermin to be eliminated—these were not aberrations from Darwinian thinking. They were its children, claiming its name and citing its texts. When you tell a civilization that it is the product of the survival of the fittest, do not be surprised when it begins to act accordingly.

Moral relativism is the inevitable harvest of evolutionary soil. If there is no Designer, there is no design. If there is no design, there is no purpose. If there is no purpose, there is no standard. And if there is no standard, then every man becomes his own measure, every culture its own law, every power its own justification. We are watching this unfold around us in real time.

**The Dignity of the Created**

Creation tells a different story—and it is the true one. You were made. You were intended. You bear the stamp of a Maker. Your mind is not a cosmic accident but a reflection of the Mind that framed the universe. Your moral sense is not an evolutionary illusion but a compass pointing to a real moral order. Your worth is not negotiable, not conferred by governments, not dependent on your utility. It is intrinsic, because it is given.

This is the foundation of human rights. This is the ground of human equality. This is the soil from which every humane civilization has grown. Strip it away, and the structures built upon it—however long they may stand by sheer inertia—will eventually fall.

**A Call to Courage**

So I call upon you tonight to have the courage to follow the evidence. To reject the state creed of naturalism. To teach your students that information requires an intelligence, that life requires a Source, that design requires a Designer. To resist the bullying of institutions that have confused consensus with truth and credentials with wisdom.

Creation is not a religious retreat from science. It is the conclusion that science, honestly pursued, demands. And it is the only foundation on which the dignity of the human person can stand.

The evidence is in. The verdict is clear. Life was created. And the sooner we return to that truth, the sooner we can rebuild a science, a culture, and a civilization worthy of the creatures we truly are.

Thank you.

---

# The Case for Creation: Why Life Bears the Mark of a Designer

**Thesis:** Life on Earth was created in its essential forms by an intelligent Creator, and the evidence from biology, information theory, and the fossil record points decisively to design rather than undirected natural processes.

## The Problem of Biological Information

Every living cell contains DNA—a digital code of staggering complexity. The human genome alone contains roughly 3 billion base pairs arranged in precise sequences that direct the construction of proteins, regulate gene expression, and coordinate cellular processes. In every other context we encounter, coded information traces back to a mind. Written languages, computer programs, and engineering blueprints all originate from intelligent authors. It is reasonable to conclude that the most sophisticated information-storage and information-processing system ever discovered likewise points to an intelligent source.

Unguided chemistry has never been shown to produce functional, specified information. Random mutations do not write new genetic instructions any more than random keystrokes write novels. The origin of biological information is best explained by an intelligent cause.

## Irreducible Complexity in Living Systems

Many biological systems exhibit what biochemist Michael Behe has termed "irreducible complexity"—multiple, precisely-matched components that must all be present for the system to function at all. The bacterial flagellum, the blood-clotting cascade, and the molecular machinery of cellular transport require numerous interdependent parts working in concert. Remove one component, and the system fails entirely.

A gradual, step-by-step process cannot build such systems, because the intermediate stages would confer no survival advantage to be selected. Only foresight—planning toward a goal—can assemble interdependent components into a functioning whole. Foresight is the hallmark of design.

## The Fossil Record and the Absence of Gradualism

If life arose through slow, gradual modification, we should expect the fossil record to be replete with transitional forms documenting the smooth transformation of one body plan into another. Instead, the fossil record shows the opposite. The Cambrian explosion reveals the sudden appearance of nearly all major animal phyla, fully formed, with no ancestral lineages in the earlier strata. Major groups appear abruptly, persist largely unchanged ("stasis"), and then disappear. This pattern is precisely what we would expect if organisms were created as distinct kinds.

## The Origin of Life Problem

Natural selection cannot account for the origin of life itself, because selection requires self-replicating organisms to already exist. Yet the gap between non-living chemistry and the simplest living cell is immense. A minimal cell requires membranes, replicating genetic material, protein machinery, and an integrated metabolic system—all functioning together from the start. Decades of origin-of-life research have only deepened the mystery, revealing more obstacles than solutions. Life's beginning points to a Life-Giver.

## The Fine-Tuning of Biological Existence

Life depends on an astonishing array of fine-tuned conditions: the properties of water, the unique chemistry of carbon, the specific parameters of Earth's orbit and atmosphere, and the precise physical constants of the universe itself. Each of these parameters falls within narrow life-permitting ranges. The cumulative improbability of these conditions arising by chance is overwhelming. Design is the natural explanation.

## Conclusion

The evidence from the information content of DNA, the irreducible complexity of biological machines, the sudden appearance of fully-formed life in the fossil record, the insurmountable problem of life's origin, and the exquisite fine-tuning of biological existence all converge on a single conclusion: life was created. The elegance, complexity, and purposeful arrangement observable throughout the living world reflect the work of an intelligent Creator who brought forth life in its essential forms. To recognize design in nature is not to abandon reason but to follow the evidence wherever it leads.

# The Case for Creation: Why Life Bears the Mark of a Designer

**Thesis:** Life on Earth is best explained by intentional creation rather than undirected evolutionary processes. The complexity, information content, and fine-tuning observed in living systems point decisively to a Creator, and the creationist account provides a more coherent explanation for the origin of life than Darwinian evolution.

## The Problem of Specified Complexity

Living organisms exhibit what philosopher William Dembski calls "specified complexity"—patterns that are both highly improbable and conform to an independent functional specification. A single bacterial cell contains molecular machines, such as the ATP synthase motor and the bacterial flagellum, whose intricate coordination rivals anything human engineers have designed. When we encounter complex, purposeful arrangements elsewhere—a watch, a computer, a cathedral—we rightly infer a designer. Consistency demands we apply the same reasoning to biological systems that vastly exceed human engineering in sophistication.

## The Information Problem

DNA is not merely a chemical; it is a code. It carries digital, symbolic information with syntax, semantics, and error-correction mechanisms. In every other domain of human experience, information traces back to an intelligent source. Natural processes produce order (crystals) or randomness (noise), but never the kind of functionally specified information that pervades the genome. Stephen Meyer's argument in *Signature in the Cell* stands largely unanswered: no undirected mechanism has been demonstrated to produce the quantity and quality of biological information required for life. Creation, by contrast, offers a sufficient cause—an intelligent mind—for an effect that is itself informational.

## Irreducible Complexity Defies Gradualism

Darwinian evolution requires that every complex structure be built through a long series of functional intermediates, each conferring a survival advantage. Yet many biological systems are irreducibly complex: remove any component and the entire system ceases to function. The blood-clotting cascade, the eukaryotic cilium, and the vertebrate immune system all depend on multiple interacting parts that have no apparent function in isolation. ### Delivered by Dr. Arthur Vance

---

Ladies and gentlemen, colleagues, friends,

I have spent forty years at the laboratory bench. I have sequenced genomes, cultured cells, traced the elegant choreography of proteins folding into their appointed shapes. And I stand before you tonight to say what the evidence has compelled me to say: life was created. It did not evolve. It was designed, fashioned, and set into motion by an intelligence vastly greater than our own. I will not soften this claim. I will not qualify it. I will defend it.

**The Testimony of Irreducible Complexity**

Consider the bacterial flagellum—a rotary engine of some forty precision-machined protein components, spinning at tens of thousands of revolutions per minute, reversing direction in a quarter-turn. Remove the stator, and it fails. Remove the rotor, and it fails. Remove the drive shaft, the bushings, the export apparatus—each removal yields not a simpler working motor but a useless heap of molecules. Natural selection cannot assemble what confers no advantage until complete. This motor was built. The blood-clotting cascade, the ribosome, the photosynthetic reaction center—each tells the same story. These are machines, and machines require machinists.

**The Testimony of the Living Fossils**

Look to the coelacanth. Look to the horseshoe crab, the nautilus, the ginkgo tree, the tuatara. Here are creatures the textbooks paraded through hundreds of millions of years of supposed evolutionary history, yet they appear in the ancient strata and in our oceans today bearing the same essential form. Where is the transformation? Where is the relentless pressure of selection that supposedly sculpts all life? These creatures stand as silent witnesses against the evolutionary narrative. They did not change because they were not evolving. They were created, each after its kind, and they have persisted as they were made. The evolutionist asks us to believe in a force so powerful it turned fish into philosophers, yet so feeble it left the coelacanth untouched in the depths for ages beyond counting. I do not believe it. The evidence does not support it.

**The Testimony of the Fossil Record**

Darwin demanded transitional forms in their countless thousands. What do the rocks deliver? The Cambrian Explosion—where nearly every major animal body plan erupts into the record, fully formed, without ancestors, without gradual lineages, without the patient branching tree the theory requires. This is not evolution's signature. This is the signature of creation: distinct kinds, appearing complete, exactly as a Designer would produce them.

**The Testimony of Information**

Within every cell lies a library. Three billion letters of code in the human genome, written in a four-letter alphabet, organized into genes, regulated by promoters and enhancers, proofread by enzymes, repaired by molecular editors. This is language. This is software. And in all of human experience, in every instance we have ever observed, information of this kind arises from one source and one source only: a mind. To claim that the most sophisticated code in the known universe wrote itself is not science. It is a faith claim, and a desperate one.

**The Testimony of Fine-Tuning—The Final Answer to Chance**

And here, my friends, is where the appeal to chance collapses utterly. The evolutionist and the materialist have always had one last refuge: given enough time, given enough trials, anything might happen. I say to them tonight: the universe itself forbids it.

The gravitational constant, the cosmological constant, the strong nuclear force, the mass ratio of proton to electron, the expansion rate of the early universe—each of these is tuned to a precision that staggers comprehension. Alter the cosmological constant by one part in 10^120, and no galaxies form, no stars ignite, no chemistry is possible. Alter the strong nuclear force by a few percent, and either no elements beyond hydrogen exist, or the universe burns out in its first moments. Alter the ratio of electromagnetism to gravity, and stable, long-lived stars—the very furnaces in which the heavier elements must form—become impossible.

These are not the rough parameters of a cosmos that happened to occur. These are the exacting specifications of a cosmos that was engineered to host life. And this, I say, is the death of the chance hypothesis. For chance requires a stage upon which to operate—a universe with stable laws, habitable regions, functional chemistry. That stage itself is the product of design. Before a single molecule could have stumbled toward self-replication, the constants of physics had already been set with a precision no lottery could ever yield. Chance does not explain life because chance cannot even explain the arena in which life would have had to arise.

The materialist invokes billions of years as if time were a magician. But time is not a creative force. Time is merely the medium in which creative forces operate. Without design, more time yields more disorder, not more order. Without design, the fine-tuning of the universe is a miracle without a miracle-worker—a sentence without an author, a symphony without a composer.

**Conclusion**

My colleagues, I did not come to these conclusions easily. I came to them because the evidence demanded it. The complexity of the cell, the stasis of the living fossils, the explosion of form in the Cambrian, the information in our DNA, and above all the breathtaking precision of the cosmos itself—these are not the fingerprints of accident. They are the signature of a Creator.

We have been told for generations that science has done away with the need for a Designer. I tell you tonight that science, honestly pursued, has done the opposite. It has brought us face to face with Him.

Thank you.

---

*End of address.*
